D10 Imaging L1 Identify Atom

How a Camera Makes an Image

light from the scene passes through a lens, lands on a sensor, and becomes a grid of numbers — that grid is the picture

A camera is three stages. Light from the scene passes through the lens, which bends rays so each point in the world lands on one point of the sensor. The sensor counts photons at every cell of its grid. Those counts, processed and quantised, become the image you see on the back of the camera.

Slide the scene brightness and watch the same flow update: brighter scene means more photons per cell, the sensor records bigger numbers, the image gets lighter. The same path runs for every photograph you have ever taken.
